request.question: The online virtual world that was the subject of the Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. copyright case launched on what date?

title: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc.
Horrible Histories World
Bragg v. Linden Lab
Stevan Lieberman
Capitol Records, LLC v Vimeo, LLC
Active Worlds
Ty Girlz
Second Life
Cityspace
Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc
sentences: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. was a copyright case in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California involving a DMCA takedown notice dispute between companies that produce virtual animals on Second Life.
Ozimals filed a DMCA takedown notice, claiming that Amaretto’s horse infringed on their bunnies.
Consequently, Amaretto filed for a temporary restraining order against Linden Research, the makers of Second Life.
This was granted and held in effect as the case proceeded.
Horrible Histories World is a virtual world which is inspired by the "Horrible Histories" book series by Terry Deary.
The premise involves the player travelling back in time and experiencing history.
The game includes 5 different virtual worlds: Putrid Pirates, Rotten Romans, Terrible Tudors, Measly Middles Ages, and Awful Egyptians.
It was put together by Scholastic Children's Books and Yomego Social Media Agency, and officially launched in August 2011.
The world was built on the Huzutech platform.
The publisher reported in November 2011 that: "We now have over 36,000 registered users to the Horrible Histories virtual world.
We are continually adding new content and hopefully this will improve.
The latest additions include a new game called Rat Runs, a joke machine and daily facts."
Other planned features included an online virtual good shop, a moderated chat feature, and events where subscribers would meet Terry Deary or a historical character.

The publishing and commercial director of Scholastic Children’s Books - Lisa Edwards - said: "We are very proud of the online Horrible Histories experience we have created and are delighted to make it available to every fan, in every corner of the globe.
In this way, it more closely reflects the Pottermore model".
Bragg v. Linden Research, Inc., 487 F. Supp.
2007), was a civil action (No. 06-4925) removed to the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania in October 2006.

Linden Lab, an online virtual world service provider of "Second Life" terminated the account of a user Marc Bragg when it discovered that he had found a way to acquire virtual land at a lower-than-market price.
The user brought this suit, which was ultimately settled before a final decision was reached.

However, the District Court did decide on two issues which may be important in future virtual world litigation: that the Second Life Terms of Service’s mandatory arbitration provision was unenforceable; and that interaction with a person in a virtual world can satisfy a state’s "minimum contacts" requirement for personal jurisdiction.
Stevan Lieberman (born July 22, 1965 in Brooklyn, New York City) is an American lawyer, regarded as a legal expert in intellectual property law and patent law.
He is a partner of the Washington, D.C.-based law firm, Greenberg & Lieberman, with Michael Greenberg, established in 1996.

As part of the firm, Lieberman has been involved in hundreds of UDRP or trademark infringement disputes, and he is considered a pioneer in the technology-law areas of virtual worlds, domain names and software, cited by CNN as "among the virtual world's earning elite."

Among the first lawyers in the world to begin generating a significant revenue and client base via online virtual worlds such as Second Life; he is the co-founder and CEO of two virtual reality websites.
"Capitol Records, LLC v. Vimeo, LLC" was a 2013 copyright infringement case out of the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York.
The decision resolved cross-motions for summary judgment filed by a video-sharing service (Vimeo) and a pair of record labels.

Vimeo sought a ruling that, as a matter of law, it was entitled to safe harbor protection under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) as to a series of copyrighted videos that were uploaded to its platform; the record labels sought the opposite ruling.
Active Worlds is an online virtual world, developed by ActiveWorlds Inc., a company based in Newburyport, Massachusetts, and launched on June 28, 1995.
Users assign themselves a name, log into the Active Worlds universe, and explore 3D virtual worlds and environments that others have built.
ActiveWorlds allows users to own worlds and universes, and develop custom 3D content.
The browser has web browsing capabilities, voice chat, and basic instant messaging.
Ty Girlz are girl dolls manufactured by Ty Inc. Similar to the Ty Beanie Babies the Ty Girlz are on a limited release pattern with dolls being introduced and older ones retired at various times.
Ty Girlz are plush toys that are connected to an online virtual world at TyGirlz.com.
Introduced to the global market on April 13, 2007, Ty Girlz one of only very few virtual worlds geared to girls when it launched.
The line was discontinued in 2013, and the virtual world shut down on June 7 of that same year.
Second Life is an online virtual world, developed and owned by the San Francisco-based firm Linden Lab and launched on June 23, 2003.
By 2013, "Second Life" had approximately 1 million regular users.

In many ways, "Second Life" is similar to massively multiplayer online role-playing games; however, Linden Lab is emphatic that their creation is not a game: "There is no manufactured conflict, no set objective".
CitySpace was an internet-based virtual world launched at SIGGRAPH 1993 by educator and project director Zane Vella.
CitySpace was one of the earliest online virtual 3D environments and first came to attention via mainstream news media in late 1993.

CitySpace was also the first user-generated virtual world, similar to virtual worlds like Second Life, and enabled participants to contribute 3D computer graphics and digital imagery to a collaborative real-time rendered 3D virtual world in which participants interacted with each other via avatars.
CitySpace was active from 1993-1996 and won the 1996 NII Award for Arts and Entertainment.
Arista Records, LLC v. LAUNCH Media, Inc., 578 F.3d 148 (2d Cir.

N.Y. 2009), is a legal case brought by Arista Records, LLC, Bad Boy Records, BMG Music, and Zomba Recording LLC (collectively, "BMG") alleging that the webcasting service provided by LAUNCH Media, Inc. ("Launch") willfully infringed BMG’s sound recording copyrights.
The lawsuit concerns the scope of the statutory term “interactive service” codified in 17 U.S.C. § 114, as amended by the Digital Millennium Copyright Act of 1998 ("DMCA").

If the webcasting service is an interactive service, Launch would be required to pay individual licensing fees to BMG’s sound recording copyright holders; otherwise, Launch only need to pay “a statutory licensing fee set by the Copyright Royalty Board.”

title: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc.
Second Life

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: The online virtual world that was the subject of the Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. copyright case launched on what date?
hop1_docs: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. | Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. was a copyright case in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California involving a DMCA takedown notice dispute between companies that produce virtual animals on Second Life.  Ozimals filed a DMCA takedown notice, claiming that Amaretto’s horse infringed on their bunnies.  Consequently, Amaretto filed for a temporary restraining order against Linden Research, the makers of Second Life.  This was granted and held in effect as the case proceeded.

Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc | Arista Records, LLC v. LAUNCH Media, Inc., 578 F.3d 148 (2d Cir.  N.Y. 2009), is a legal case brought by Arista Records, LLC, Bad Boy Records, BMG Music, and Zomba Recording LLC (collectively, "BMG") alleging that the webcasting service provided by LAUNCH Media, Inc. ("Launch") willfully infringed BMG’s sound recording copyrights.  The lawsuit concerns the scope of the statutory term “interactive service” codified in 17 U.S.C. § 114, as amended by the Digital Millennium Copyright Act of 1998 ("DMCA").  If the webcasting service is an interactive service, Launch would be required to pay individual licensing fees to BMG’s sound recording copyright holders; otherwise, Launch only need to pay “a statutory licensing fee set by the Copyright Royalty Board.”

Capitol Records, Inc. v. MP3Tunes, LLC | Capitol Records, Inc. v. MP3tunes, LLC is a 2011 case from the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York concerning copyright infringement and the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA).  In the case, EMI Music Group and fourteen other record companies (including Capitol Records) claimed copyright infringement against MP3tunes, which provides online music storage lockers, and MP3tunes's founder, Michael Robertson.  In a decision that has ramifications for the future of online locker services, the court held that MP3tunes qualifies for safe harbor protection under the DMCA.  However, the court found MP3tunes to still be liable for contributory copyright infringement in this case due to its failure to remove infringing songs after receiving takedown notices.  The court also held that Robertson is liable for songs he personally copied from unauthorized websites.

There (virtual world) | There is a 3D online virtual world created by Will Harvey and Jeffrey Ventrella.  There Inc. was founded in the spring of 1998.  Closed beta began in July 2001, with various stages of beta following, and ending with an October 2003 launch date.  On March 9, 2010 - one week after the announcement of its closure on March 2, 2010 - There.com shut its doors to the public.

Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. | Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. was a 2013 case from the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York concerning copyright infringement of digital music.  In "ReDigi", record label Capitol Records claimed copyright infringement against ReDigi, a service that allows resale of digital music tracks originally purchased from the iTunes Store.  Capitol Records' motion for a preliminary injunction against ReDigi was denied, and oral arguments were given on October 5, 2012.

Active Worlds | Active Worlds is an online virtual world, developed by ActiveWorlds Inc., a company based in Newburyport, Massachusetts, and launched on June 28, 1995.  Users assign themselves a name, log into the Active Worlds universe, and explore 3D virtual worlds and environments that others have built.  ActiveWorlds allows users to own worlds and universes, and develop custom 3D content.  The browser has web browsing capabilities, voice chat, and basic instant messaging.

Righthaven LLC v. Democratic Underground LLC | Righthaven LLC.  v. Democratic Underground LLC was a copyright infringement case which determined that a contract giving a party right to sue on behalf of a copyright holder does not give the party legal standing to file such lawsuits.  This case is one of over 200 similar cases filed by Righthaven against media outlets using content from Stephens Media.  Judge Roger L. Hunt ruled that Righthaven lacked standing to file a copyright infringement suit and ordered Righthaven to show cause within two weeks why it should not be sanctioned for failure to disclose Stephens Media as an interested party.

Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc
Capitol Records, Inc. v. MP3Tunes, LLC
Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.
Righthaven LLC v. Democratic Underground LLC
response.summary_1: Passage [1]: case involved virtual animals on Second Life. Bridge/missing fact: the launch date of Second Life. Retrieval plan: 1) Open the "Second Life" Wikipedia page and check the infobox (Initial release) and the "History"/"Launch" section for the launch date; if needed 2) search "Second Life launch date site:lindenlab.com OR site:wikipedia.org" or fetch Linden Lab press releases announcing Second Life's launch.
hop2_docs: Life 24 | Life 24 was a British television channel owned and operated by Life TV Media, it was the second television channel from the company.  Life TV Media had also launched three other channels to complement Life 24; Life TV, Life Showcase TV and Life One.  It was launched on 24 July 2006.  and closed on 20 August 2007

Second Life | Second Life is an online virtual world, developed and owned by the San Francisco-based firm Linden Lab and launched on June 23, 2003.  By 2013, "Second Life" had approximately 1 million regular users.  In many ways, "Second Life" is similar to massively multiplayer online role-playing games; however, Linden Lab is emphatic that their creation is not a game: "There is no manufactured conflict, no set objective".

Movies OK | Movies OK is a Hindi movie television channel broadcast in India.  It is owned by Star TV and Fox International Channels.  The channel was launched on 6 May 2012.  It is Star's second Hindi movie channel, the first being STAR Gold which was launched in 2000.  It is also the second channel to be branded under the "OK" brand rather than the "STAR" brand after Life OK, a general entertainment channel which was launched in December 2011 to replace STAR One.  Before the launch of Movies OK, STAR acquired Viacom 18's movie portfolio, paving the way for the launch of the new channel.  From OK Series Of Flagship Channel - Life OK Renamed of STAR Bharat, Later Movies OK Will be Replaced Of STAR Bharat Movies.

Split capital investment trust | A split capital investment trust (split) is a type of investment trust which issues different classes of share to give the investor a choice of shares to match their needs.  Most splits have a limited life determined at launch known as the wind-up date.  Typically the life of a split capital trust is five to ten years.

Lust for Life (Iggy Pop album) | Lust for Life is the second studio album by Iggy Pop, his second solo release and his second collaboration with David Bowie, following "The Idiot" earlier in the year.  As well as achieving critical success, it was Pop's most commercially popular album to date, and remains his only Gold-certified release in the UK.

Proton Saga (second generation) | The second generation Proton Saga is a 4-door subcompact sedan produced by Malaysian car manufacturer Proton which launched on 18 January 2008.  It succeeds the first generation Proton Saga as the company's flagship model.  The second generation Proton Saga is based on a stretched Proton Savvy platform and was developed in-house by Proton with technical support from Lotus Cars and LG CNS.  Proton launched a mid- life facelift model called the Proton Saga FL on 30 November 2010.  8 months later, a further improved model called the Proton Saga FLX was launched.

BuzMuzik | BuzMuzik was a short-lived British music television channel owned and operated by CSC Media Group.  Unlike MinX, another short lived music channel by CSC, its run lasted for two years compared to the other channel's 1 month life.  It launched on 30 May 2012, the launch date having slipped back several times from an initially mooted February opening.
response.summary_2: The virtual world was Second Life. Passage [2] (Second Life) explicitly states it was 'launched on June 23, 2003' (supporting the launch date); no other retrieved passages supply relevant metadata.
